topics: 🐂 שׁוֹר שֶׁנָּגַח אֶת הָאָדָם — the goring bull and what its owner pays
💬 R' Eliezer vs. R' Akiva: what does וּבַעַל הַשּׁוֹר נָקִי exempt?
🪦 R' Akiva's challenge: is the owner not 'destined for the stoning place'?
📖 R' Eliezer's narrowing: only when the bull intended to kill an animal
🔁 Gezerah shavah: הַשּׁוֹר יִסָּקֵל — does it learn from סָקוֹל יִסָּקֵל or from itself?

overview.title: 📖 Overview — What 'The Owner Is Free' Really Means
overview.label: The sugya:
overview.text: Exodus 21:28 says that when an ox gores a person to death, the ox is stoned and 'the owner of the bull is free' (וּבַעַל הַשּׁוֹר נָקִי). R' Eliezer reads this as freeing him from half the kofer (weregilt). R' Akiva pushes back: the owner is already destined for the stoning place — how can the verse be talking about him? The Yerushalmi tracks both opinions through five distinct moves: a frontal challenge, a narrowing, an internal critique, alternative readings of the exemption, and a closing gezerah shavah about which version of 'the bull shall be stoned' anchors the law.
overview.hebrewSource: וּבַעַל הַשּׁוֹר נָקִי. נָקִי מֵחֲצִי כוֹפֶר. דִּבְרֵי רִבִּי אֱלִיעֶזֶר. אָמַר לוֹ רִבִּי עֲקִיבָה. רִבִּי. וַהֲלֹא מוּקְדָּם הוּא לְבֵית הַסְקִילָה חֲמוּרָה

slides.title: 💬 R' Eliezer vs. R' Akiva — Round One
slides.subtitle: What does the Torah free the owner from?

lines.speaker: RE
lines.text: וּבַעַל הַשּׁוֹר נָקִי — free from half the kofer. When the bull is a tam that kills a person, the owner is exempt from paying half-weregilt.

lines.speaker: RA
lines.text: רִבִּי, וַהֲלֹא מוּקְדָּם הוּא לְבֵית הַסְקִילָה חֲמוּרָה? — Rabbi, isn't he already destined for the severity of the stoning place? How can the verse even discuss money?

lines.speaker: RE
lines.text: לֹא אָמַרְתִּי אֶלָּא בְּנִתְכַּוֵון לַהֲרוֹג אֶת הַבְּהֵמָה וְהָרַג אֶת הָאָדָם — I only spoke about a case where the bull intended to kill an animal but ended up killing a person. Then there is no death penalty for the owner, and the kofer-exemption is meaningful.

callout.label: Key move:
callout.text: R' Akiva is not denying the exemption. He is pointing out that for a typical murderous-bull case, the owner faces capital liability anyway — so the verse can't be his concern. R' Eliezer answers by carving out the unintended-killing case where the owner avoids the stoning court.

slides.title: 🔎 The Yerushalmi's Internal Critique
slides.subtitle: Should R' Akiva's question apply just as much to himself?

nodes.content:
R' Akiva attacks R' Eliezer:
The owner is already going to בית הסקילה.

עַד דּוּ מַקְשֵׁי לָהּ עַל דְּרִבִּי לִעֶזֶר יַקְשֶׁינָּהּ עַל דִּידֵיהּ
Before he objects to R' Eliezer, shouldn't he object to himself?

columns.label: R' Miasha
columns.content:
תִּיפְתָּר בְּשֶׁעָבַר וּשְׁחָטוֹ
Read it about a case where he transgressed and slaughtered the bull — so no stoning court for the owner here either.

columns.label: Alternative reading
columns.content: R' Akiva himself uses the same narrowing as R' Eliezer: the case is when the bull intended to kill an animal, or a Gentile, or a stillbirth.

nodes.content: Either way, R' Akiva's exemption — like R' Eliezer's — only operates outside the standard capital case.

callout.label: Yerushalmi style:
callout.text: The Yerushalmi will not let an Amora score points cheaply. If R' Akiva's question would also undermine his own reading, the Yerushalmi forces him to live with the same answer.

slides.title: 🆚 Two Readings of וּבַעַל הַשּׁוֹר נָקִי
slides.subtitle: Each Tanna assigns the exemption to a different liability.

boxes.title: 🐂 R' Eliezer
items: נָקִי מֵחֲצִי כוֹפֶר — free from half the weregilt
Operative case: tam that intended an animal but killed a person
Verse אִם כּוֹפֶר יוּשַׁת עָלָיו speaks of the mu'ad — full kofer there
So 'נקי' here must address the tam — exempt from half

boxes.title: 📜 R' Akiva
items: נָקִי מִדְּמֵי עֶבֶד — free from paying for a slave
Worried about the stoning court collision
Reads the exemption against the slave-payment rule of Ex. 21:32
Forces the case into the same narrow scenarios R' Eliezer used

boxes.title: 🤝 Common ground
items: Both agree the verse cannot mean a standard murderous-bull case
Both narrow it to where the owner is not capitally liable
They differ only on which monetary payment the verse waives

callout.label: Why the dispute matters:
callout.text: It's not a vague hermeneutic preference. R' Eliezer needs נָקִי for half-kofer because the verse אִם כּוֹפֶר יוּשַׁת already covers the mu'ad's full kofer. R' Akiva reroutes the exemption to the slave-payment because in his system the half-kofer is handled differently.

slides.title: 📊 Where Does Each Side Get Its Other Exemption?
slides.subtitle: Each Tanna must source the exemption the other reads into נקי.
rows: Yes — directly from וּבַעַל הַשּׁוֹר נָקִי
Yes — derived via gezerah shavah of הַשּׁוֹר יִסָּקֵל from הַשּׁוֹר יִסָּקֵל
Free from paying for a slave?
Yes — derived from אִם כּוֹפֶר יוּשַׁת עָלָיו (mu'ad context)
Yes — directly from וּבַעַל הַשּׁוֹר נָקִי
The kofer-imposition verse speaks of mu'ad, leaving נקי for the tam's half-kofer
The two phrasings of 'the bull shall be stoned' link tam to mu'ad's law
נֶאֱמַר כָּאן הַשּׁוֹר יִסָּקֵל וְנֶאֱמַר לְהַלָּן הַשּׁוֹר יִסָּקֵל

callout.label: Important:
callout.text: Both Tannaim arrive at all the same exemptions. Their dispute is purely about which words of Torah carry which job.

slides.title: 🧠 The Closing Gezerah Shavah
slides.subtitle: Which 'the bull shall be stoned' is the source — and which is the recipient?

steps.content: 1️⃣ The Torah twice uses similar phrases: הַשּׁוֹר יִסָּקֵל (in our parsha, on tam) and הַשּׁוֹר יִסָּקֵל (later, on mu'ad).
2️⃣ R' Akiva: just as 'the bull shall be stoned' there speaks of the mu'ad, so 'the bull shall be stoned' here also gets read in the mu'ad's framework — yielding the half-kofer exemption for the tam.
3️⃣ Objection: but elsewhere the Torah writes סָקוֹל יִסָּקֵל הַשּׁוֹר — wouldn't that be the better link? Doubled language is usually weightier.
4️⃣ Resolution: מוּטָּב יִלָּמֵד הַשּׁוֹר יִסָּקֵל מִשּׁוֹר יִסָּקֵל — better that 'the bull shall be stoned' learn from a phrase that matches it exactly.
5️⃣ Principle: a gezerah shavah prefers identical phrasing over a near-identical doubled phrasing.

callout.label: Bottom line:
callout.text: The daf, which on the surface is a short three-line dispute, actually contains: a frontal Tannaitic clash, a self-objection move (יַקְשֶׁינָּהּ עַל דִּידֵיהּ), two parallel exemption-mappings, and a meta-rule about how to choose between competing gezerot shavot.

summary.principle: Both R' Eliezer and R' Akiva agree that an owner whose bull kills a person can owe money — but only in narrow non-capital scenarios. They split on which payment the words וּבַעַל הַשּׁוֹר נָקִי exempt, and the Yerushalmi maps each side's verse-by-verse logic.

flowSteps.content: What is the owner free from?

columns.content:
🔴 R' Akiva
Free from paying for a slave

🟣 Both
Only outside the capital case (intended animal, etc.)

flowSteps.content:
🎓 Key Principle:
Two Tannaim, two assignments of נָקִי, but the same monetary outcomes — because each side imports the other's exemption through a different pasuk.

quiz.q: According to R' Eliezer, what does וּבַעַל הַשּׁוֹר נָקִי exempt the owner from?
o: From paying full damages
From half the kofer (weregilt)
From the death penalty itself
From watching his bull
quiz.e: R' Eliezer says: נָקִי מֵחֲצִי כוֹפֶר. He reads the verse as exempting the tam-owner from half-weregilt.

quiz.q: What is R' Akiva's challenge to R' Eliezer?
o: The verse is about a fire, not a bull
וַהֲלֹא מוּקְדָּם הוּא לְבֵית הַסְקִילָה חֲמוּרָה — the owner is already going to the stoning court
Half-kofer doesn't exist as a category
The owner of a tam never pays anything
quiz.e: R' Akiva argues that in the standard case the owner faces capital prosecution, so the verse cannot be focused on his money payments.

quiz.q: How does R' Eliezer narrow the case to make his reading work?
o: Only when the bull is hekdesh
Only in cases where the owner declared the bull ownerless
Only when the bull intended to kill an animal but ended up killing a person
Only on Shabbat
quiz.e: לֹא אָמַרְתִּי אֶלָּא בְּנִתְכַּוֵון לַהֲרוֹג אֶת הַבְּהֵמָה וְהָרַג אֶת הָאָדָם — only in non-capital scenarios. R' Akiva ends up using the same narrowing.

quiz.q: What is the meaning of the Yerushalmi's pushback יַקְשֶׁינָּהּ עַל דִּידֵיהּ?
o: He should ask his own teacher first
Before objecting to R' Eliezer, R' Akiva should ask the same question against his own position
He should learn it as a Mishnah rather than a beraita
He should let R' Miasha decide
quiz.e: The Yerushalmi notes that R' Akiva's challenge would equally undermine his own reading, so he must accept the same narrowing — either via R' Miasha's solution (בְּשֶׁעָבַר וּשְׁחָטוֹ) or by adopting R' Eliezer's qualification.

quiz.q: Why does the Yerushalmi prefer learning הַשּׁוֹר יִסָּקֵל from הַשּׁוֹר יִסָּקֵל rather than from סָקוֹל יִסָּקֵל הַשּׁוֹר?
o: Doubled verbs are always weaker
מוּטָּב יִלָּמֵד הַשּׁוֹר יִסָּקֵל מִשּׁוֹר יִסָּקֵל — exact phrasing match is preferred over a near-match
The doubled form is in a different sefer
R' Akiva rejects all gezerot shavot
quiz.e: The Yerushalmi establishes a meta-principle: when choosing between two possible source-phrases for a gezerah shavah, the one with identical phrasing wins over the doubled-language version.
